title: PHOENIX
text: PHOENIX by Mark Allan Gunnells "Look at that,” Sheryl said, sticking her arm past Frank as she pointed emphatically out the driver’s side window. Frank jerked, causing the car to swerve to the left before he pulled it back in line. “Honey, are you trying to make me drive us off the road? Why don’t you just punch me in the neck and grab the steering wheel next time?” “Stop being so melodramatic and look .” Frank followed her pointing finger just as they passed a narrow dirt road that disappeared into a wooded area. Next to the road was a large sign that read: PHOENIX DRIVE-IN OPEN NIGHTLY AT 7 TONIGHT’S DOUBLE FEATURE EAST OF EDEN & REBEL WITHOUT A CAUSE Frank just grunted as the sign disappeared behind them. “What are you doing, turn around,” Sheryl said. “For what?” “It’s 6:50, we can just make the first show.” “Surely you’re not serious.” “I surely am, and my name isn’t Shirley.” “That’s not how that quote goes, you know.” “Whatever. I’ve never been to a drive-in before.” “Well, I have. My parents used to take me and my little brother to a drive-in when we were kids, and it wasn’t that much fun. Give me a nice roomy theater with comfy stadium seating any day.” “Oh, come on Frank, be a sport. I’ve always wanted to go to a drive-in but I didn’t think there were any left.” “Look, you wanted to take the scenic route back from your Aunt Belinda’s instead of the interstate which would have gotten us home much quicker, and I said fine, but I am not going to stop on the way and watch two movies. It’s going to be after midnight before we get home as it is.” “Well, we don’t have to stay for both films, we can just watch the first one then take the interstate the rest of the way.” “Sheryl, I’m really not in the mood for a movie.” “But it’s James Dean, you love James Dean.” “Yeah, and I have all his movies on Blu-ray back at the house.” “But that’s not the same as seeing it on the big screen. It’ll be worth it, trust me. I’ll make it worth your while.” “What are you talking about?” “I’ve always heard that drive-ins could be a lot of … fun for teenagers.” “Sheryl, we’re not teenagers.” “We could be tonight.” Frank looked over at his wife with a frown, as if he wasn’t sure who was sitting next to him and how she’d gotten in his car. “What are getting at?” Sheryl scooted closer, placing a hand on Frank’s thigh. “You know that thing you like, the thing I’m rarely in the mood for? Well, I just might be in the mood for it tonight.” “Are you serious?” “We could park somewhere near the back of the lot…might be exciting.” “What’s gotten into you?” Sheryl let her hand stray further up her husband’s thigh then said teasingly, “What, not interested?” Frank was interested all right, and if Sheryl’s hand went much higher she’d find out just how interested he really was, but he decided to tease her back just a bit. “Oh, I don’t know. I’d be all messy, I’d hate to have to drive all the way back home like that.” Leaning forward, Sheryl whispered in his ear, “There wouldn’t necessarily be any mess.” “You don’t mean…?” She nodded. “ If we stay for both movies.” Frank didn’t say anything, but he flipped on his signal when he spotted a BP gas station up ahead on the right. He turned around in the parking lot then headed back toward the Phoenix. # The ticket booth was a couple of yards down the dirt road on the left. Behind the glass was a teenage girl with a blonde ponytail and dead eyes. She didn’t seem to notice the car at first, and when she did she seemed surprised, as if not expecting any more customers this close to show time. She slid open the window, stared for a moment, then said in a voice as lifeless as her eyes, “You’re new.” Frank didn’t know what to say to that so he just stared back. Finally the girl pointed to a sign tacked to the front of the booth that read “1 Dollar per Carload.” Frank frowned; surely that couldn’t be right. But when he handed over a five, the girl gave him four ones as change. He thanked her, and as he pulled away from the booth he thought he heard her say, “You shouldn’t have stopped.” “Well, she was a friendly sort, wasn’t she?” he grumbled. Sheryl shrugged. “Probably sore she has to work on a Saturday night while her friends no doubt are enjoying the double-feature.” Frank coasted along, the branches of the trees that lined either side of the dirt road reaching across to create a virtual ceiling above the car. It wasn’t yet full dark, but the foliage gave the impression that Frank was driving through a tunnel. At least until he rounded a slight curve and ended up in a clearing that contained the dirt lot as well as the massive white screen and a concession stand at the far end. The area was large, with enough spaces for at least fifty cars, most of which were already occupied. “Whoa,” Frank said, stopping at the entrance to the very back row. Sheryl glanced out the windshield. “Popular place.” “Yeah, not to mention…peculiar.” “What do you mean?” “Sheryl, take a good look around. At the cars, the people. Don’t you notice anything a little strange?” Sheryl leaned forward and studied the drive-in. Frank saw the realization dawn in her eyes and she grunted a puzzled, “Hmpf.” It wasn’t the sight of so many cars that gave one pause, but the type of cars. From what Frank could see, his 2019 Camry was the only vehicle in the lot that wasn’t at least fifty or sixty years old. All the cars were older models, the large boxy types that looked as if they could house families of four or more. But despite their age, the vehicles looked to be in near-mint condition. And yet even more peculiar than the cars were the people. Groups milled about outside their vehicles, flitting from car to car to visit and chat. Most of them were teenagers, which wasn’t so unusual, but what was unusual was how they were dressed. The girls were wearing mostly jumpers or poodle skirts and sweaters. The boys fell into one of two camps: tight jeans, leather jackets, greasy hair done up in exaggerated pompadours, or preppied out in slacks and button-up shirts, short hair combed with precision. Frank felt like he’d stepped into that old movie Pleasantville , which in turn was about stepping into an old TV show. “Let’s do the time warp again,” he said softly. Beside him, Sheryl clapped her hands together and giggled delightedly. “Oh, they must be having a theme night. How fun.” “Theme night?” “Sure. Since they’re showing the James Dean movies, they must be having 50s night, probably advertised it in the paper or something. That would explain the cheap admission price and the outfits.” Frank scanned the lot again. “But what about the cars? Do you really think all these kids just happened to have access to vintage cars?” Sheryl dismissed the question with a shrug. “It looks like we picked a good night to find this place.” Frank couldn’t help but smile at his wife’s enthusiasm and excitement. Her promise had him pretty excited himself. He drove down the back row to the very last space and pulled in. The spots around him were unoccupied and he hoped they stayed that way. He stared out at the metal pole next to the car. “That’s odd.” “What?” Sheryl said, running her fingers up and down his forearm, something she knew drove him wild. “Um, just that most drive-ins these days pipe the sound in through the car radio, but this place still has the old-timey speakers you perch on your window.” “Well, I think we should keep the windows rolled up for a while. They might even get all fogged over.” Again Frank looked at his wife as if she were a stranger, but one he very much wanted to get to know. “If I’d had any idea going to a drive-in would have this effect on you, I’d have taken you years ago.” “Maybe we’ll have to become repeat customers.” “I could be up for that.” Sheryl placed a hand on his crotch and squeezed. “I’ll say.” On the far side of the lot, the towering screen exploded to life in a blaze of color and sound, and Frank leaned his head against the back of the seat as his wife unzipped his pants. # Twenty minutes later, Frank wiped sweat from his forehead with the back of a hand, panting for breath as if he’d just run five miles. “Sweet Jesus,” he gasped. “I mean, that was just…sweet Jesus.” Next to him, Sheryl was digging through her purse. She finally found a tissue and wiped away her smudged lipstick then smiled at her husband. “Glad you enjoyed it. Now I could use some water.” “What?” “Water. I suddenly have the overwhelming desire to rinse out my mouth.” “Want me to go grab a bottle from the concession stand?” “I’d say it’s the least you can do after all I did for you.” “Hey, no argument from me,” Frank said, tucking himself in and zipping up. “Want anything else while I’m going?” “Well, since we’re going to be staying for both films, you might as well get us some sodas and popcorn. Biggest, butteriest tub they’ve got.” “I’m on it.” Frank opened the door and stepped out then turned and leaned back into the car. “You’re amazing, you know that?” “Yeah, I know,” Sheryl said, flicking her tongue out playfully. “I just have to remind you of it every once in a while.” “Maybe during the second movie I’ll show my appreciation by returning the favor.” “Now that you’ve put it out there, I’m going to hold you to it.” Frank closed the car door and started across the lot in a daze. He couldn’t believe that he’d just gotten a blowjob in his car in a public arena from his wife of nearly ten years. It was the kind of thing they would have done in their younger days, and in fact it left Frank feeling more youthful than he had in years. He glanced at the screen, realizing he hadn’t paid the slightest attention to the movie since it had started. James Dean was up there being all moody and angst-ridden, winning the affection of his brother’s girl. This was actually Frank’s favorite James Dean picture, but he didn’t regret missing the beginning. The show in his Camry had been much more engrossing. As he neared the concession stand, Frank’s nose prickled as the sharp odor of gasoline assaulted his nostrils. He stopped in his tracks and looked around until he spotted the source. A Plymouth Savoy with Belvedere trim sat in a spot close by, and there must have been a crack in the gas tank because it was dribbling fuel in a puddle that was starting to run out from under the car in a small river. Concerned, Frank stepped up to the car to alert the driver, but he peered through the half-opened window with the speaker affixed to it to find the vehicle empty. He took a moment to admire the restoration job to this classic; even the leather of the seats smelled fairly new. Frank stepped back and scanned the immediate area but saw no one that looked as if they might belong to this car. Glancing uneasily at the spreading puddle of gas, he hurried on to the concession stand. There were only two kids in line in front of him so he didn’t have to wait long. He ordered a large tub of buttered corn and two small sodas, noting that the 50s theme extended to ridiculously low prices for the food and beverages. Before leaving with the goodies, he pointed back over his shoulder and said to the pimply faced boy working the counter, “There’s a car leaking gas back there.” The boy’s eyes were wide with a haunted quality, and Frank was suddenly afraid he was going to cry. Instead the boy dropped his head, staring down at the sticky countertop, and said in a voice so soft and breathy that Frank really had to strain to make out the words, “It’s always leaking. There’s nothing we can do to stop it.” Frank didn’t know how to respond to that so he just gathered up his items and started back to the car. As he approached the Plymouth he saw that there was now a young man leaning against the back of it with his left foot propped on the tire. He wore a red jacket not unlike the one worn by Dean in Rebel , jeans so tight they were surely killing off any chance he had to father children in the future, his pompadour impressive in its height. He looked to be eighteen or nineteen, a look of practiced indifference on his face. Someone whose idea of cool was to seem as if he didn’t care whether or not he looked cool. The cigarette he puffed on completed the picture. “Hey,” Frank called out, noticing that the young man was standing with his right foot in the puddle of gasoline but apparently oblivious to it. “Watch out! You’ve got a gas leak!” The boy glanced at Frank, the cigarette dangling from his lips. Frank noticed how the boy’s trembling mouth made the cigarette shake and jitter. The boy sprung a leak himself, a few tears trailing down his cheek, ruining his “too cool for school” vibe. “I know,” he said. “Happens every night. It’s my fault, always my fault. Maybe this is my punishment.” Confused, Frank opened his mouth to tell the boy to get the hell away from the gas with that cigarette, but at that moment a perky redheaded girl whose pink sweater was working overtime to constrain her ample bosom walked up to the boy in the “Rebel” jacket. Natalie Wood to his James Dean. The young man smiled with one corner of his mouth even as tears continued to trickle down his face then, without looking down, flicked the still-burning cigarette to the ground. To Frank, it seemed the butt fell in slow motion, reality stretching out like silly putty. He thought maybe he tried to shout a warning but he heard no sound. He was frozen, watching the events unfold as if this was just a movie up on the screen. The cigarette landed and ignited the gas instantly, a large whoosh of flame that engulfed the young man’s right leg. He certainly lost his cool then, letting loose with a scream so high-pitched Frank was surprised the windows of all the nearby cars didn’t shatter. The girl in the too small sweater also screamed and back-peddled quickly, tripping over her feet and toppling into the dirt. Time resumed its regular course at that point…or, it seemed to Frank, perhaps it speeded up a bit. He dropped the snacks and started forward to try to help the young man, but then the Plymouth caught fire and the gas tank exploded. The force knocked Frank onto his back, and the young man’s screams ended…only to be replaced by a multitude of screams throughout the lot. Frank pushed up on his elbows and looked back toward the Plymouth…or at least where the Plymouth had been. The explosion had apparently sent it airborne and the burning hulk of the car was now resting upside down on top of a Ford Thunderbird. People were running about like human torches, and the stench of sizzling flesh was thick as the smoke. While Frank watched, the Thunderbird also exploded, sending shrapnel through the air that took out several more people. The screen itself had been hit by some of the debris and was on fire, the flames eating up James Dean and Julie Harris. A few panicked moviegoers tried to bolt in their cars but ended up crashing into one another. “Sheryl,” Frank whispered, scrambling to his feet and sprinting across the lot toward where his Camry was parked. Behind him he heard more cars exploding in a chain reaction of destruction. He dug his keys out of his pocket to have them at the ready, but there was another explosion and a fiery Chevy sedan landed a few feet in front of him, blocking his path. He skidded to a stop, his feet slipping out from under him and sending him crashing onto his side. The keys were knocked out of his hand, flying right into the midst of the flaming Chevy. This time Frank screamed his wife’s name and he bolted to his feet, skirting around the sedan and continuing toward his car. He could see it just ahead, and through the windshield that reflected the dancing flames he could see Sheryl, her eyes wide and her mouth open in a silent scream. A girl ran across his path; he thought it might have been the redhead in the pink sweater, but it was hard to tell since both her hair and her sweater were on fire. Frank pumped his legs, trying to channel his high school self that had lettered in track. He was sweating from the exertion, but he noticed in a distracted sort of way that he felt no heat. Odd considering the roaring inferno the drive-in had become. It took him less than a minute to reach the car, but time had slowed again and it seemed to take him much longer. He jumped into the driver’s seat and screamed at his wife, “Give me your keys!” At first Sheryl didn’t respond, seemingly transfixed by the nightmare unfolding through the windshield. When she did look at her husband, her eyes were empty. “KEYS!” Frank shouted in her face. “I lost mine out there in all that mess, I need your set so we can get the hell out of here!” Sheryl’s senses seemed to return to her then. Maybe not completely, but enough. She snatched her purse up from the floorboard and dug around until coming up with her keys on the rabbit’s foot keychain. Frank snatched them, started the car, and stomped down on the gas. Luckily he’d never attached the speaker to the window; otherwise he would have ripped it off. There were more accidents near the dirt road that led out of the lot, but Frank somehow managed to weave through the poles, burning vehicles, and burning people like he was some kind of stunt driver. He sped down the road, which had now become a tunnel of flame as several of the trees were ablaze. Smoldering branches and leaves fell around the car like fiery judgment from heaven. Sheryl was gripping the dashboard, her face twisted into a mask of fear. As they approached the ticket booth, Frank saw that the flames had reached it as well, the roof burning and smoking. He wondered briefly if he should stop and check on the girl working the booth, but surely she would have noticed the fire. Just to be safe, he laid down on the horn as he shot past the booth and out onto the main road. He turned sharply to the left, tires screeching on the pavement. “Oh my god, all those people,” Sheryl said, her voice strained. “They were burning up. I think I saw a boy’s face melt off.” Frank was gulping air like water. “It’s okay, it’ll be okay. Just call 911.” Sheryl looked at him dazed for a moment, as if she didn’t understand his words. Finally she began rummaging through her purse and said, “Pull over.” “Why? You can call for help while I drive.” “Just pull over!” she snapped, and Frank did as she asked, coasting to a stop on the gravel shoulder. The police would probably need them to give a statement anyway, so best if they didn’t stray too far. Even though part of him wanted to drive straight home and forget this whole horrible night. As if that were possible. As Sheryl fumbled her phone with shaky hands, Frank wondered how a night that had started out so great had turned so tragic so fast. And he couldn’t help but blame himself. If only he’d stayed by the Plymouth after discovering the leak until the owner returned…if only he’d shouted out a warning a little sooner, a little louder… Sheryl put the phone on speaker and it rang twice before a bored older female voice answered, “911, what is the nature of your emergency?” When Sheryl didn’t respond right away, Frank leaned toward the screen and said, “Yes, we’re on Highway 11 just outside Summerville. You need to send fire trucks and ambulances to the drive-in near the BP gas station.” A pause on the line then, “Excuse me?” “Please, you need to hurry. The whole place is on fire, cars exploding, people…Jesus wept, the people…” Another pause. “What?” “Don’t you speak fucking English?” Sheryl shouted into the phone. “The drive-in theater is a firestorm. You need to get help.” “You folks talking about the Phoenix?” Frank took the phone from his wife. “Yes, the Phoenix. People are dying.” “You think you’re funny?” “What are you talking about?” Frank said. “This is no joke.” “You’re right, this is no joking matter so I don’t really appreciate your little prank.” “Prank? Lady, I’m telling you the God’s honest truth. If you send someone out to the drive-in, you’ll see.” “I don’t need to send anyone. I know all about the fire at the Phoenix.” “Did someone else call for help?” Sheryl asked. “No, we don’t usually get calls about disasters sixty-six years in the past.” Frank and his wife exchanged a puzzled glance, and Frank found himself wondering if the woman was off her rocker. The operator barked a sharp laugh that came through the phone devoid of humor. “The fire at the Phoenix drive-in was in 1956. Worst tragedy this county has ever seen, so many lives lost. The people in this area aren’t likely to forget it.” Frank shook his head. “No, no, you must be mistaken.” “I wasn’t born yet, but I lost a sister in the blaze so my parents certainly never forgot the fire. I heard about it my entire life.” “That’s not possible. We just left there.” “I’ve heard enough of this,” the woman said, biting off her words as if they were bitter in her mouth. “You know it’s a felony to prank call 911, but I’m going to let you off with a warning. You call back, though, and I’ll have your asses in jail.” With that, the line disconnected. “What was she talking about?” Sheryl said softly, her voice trembling. As was her body. “Was she crazy or what?” Frank scooted across the seat and held his wife close to him. “Had to be. She just had to be.” They sat in silence for several moments then Frank slowly turned to look out the back windshield, back toward the Phoenix. There were no flames visible, no churning smoke climbing into the dark sky. Nothing to indicate the blazing hell they’d left behind. Sheryl followed his gaze then grabbed his arm and squeezed hard enough to hurt. “What’s going on? Where’s the fire?” “I don’t know.” Frank sat there for several more minutes before pulling back onto the road, making a U-turn and heading not toward the interstate but back the way they’d come. He expected Sheryl to protest, but she remained silent. Frank almost missed the turn because the sign was no longer there. The dirt road was overgrown with weeds. The ride was bumpier than he remembered, and there was no longer that sense of going through a tunnel because there were considerably less trees. In the glare of the headlights, Frank noted a few blackened stumps. The ticket booth was gone, just some kindling scattered about the ground. Frank rounded the curve into what had been the lot and stopped suddenly. The poles were still there, some of them tilted at crazy angles and almost all of them missing the speakers. Although the screen was no more, the concession stand remained, but it was boarded up, the overhang partially collapsed. Sheryl opened the passenger’s side door and stepped out, wandering away from the car. Frank rolled down his window and stuck his head out. “Honey, get back in the car.” “This can’t be,” Sheryl said, continuing to meander across the lot. “I mean, we were just here. There was a movie playing, there were dozens of people. It was real…wasn’t it?” Reluctantly, Frank got out of the car and started after his wife. “Sheryl, please. We need to go.” Sheryl came to such an abrupt halt Frank almost ran into her back. She bent over, sifting through the dirt and came up with a set of keys that jangled in her hand. “Your keys,” she said without inflection, holding them out to her husband. Absurdly, Frank didn’t want to touch them, as if afraid contact with the keys would send him spiraling into madness. Finally he reached out and allowed Sheryl to drop them into his palm. “Come on, we have to go.” “Frank, aren’t we going to talk about what happened?” “Nothing happened. We are both just tired from the trip and…” “And what? We both had the same hallucination?” “I don’t want to talk about this now. I just want to get home.” “But Frank—” “I said I didn’t want to talk about it!” he shouted, the ferocity in his voice instantly silencing his wife. He didn’t want to talk about it, but he couldn’t stop thinking about it. Specifically how the girl in the ticket booth, the guy at the concession counter, and the “Rebel” who started everything with his cigarette seemed to be aware of their predicament, of being stuck in a repeating loop, which made it worse than any concept of hell Frank could imagine. The “Rebel” had said, “Happens every night.” So if Frank and Sheryl came back tomorrow night, would they get a repeat performance? It was almost too horrific to consider, so Frank just wanted to put it out of his mind. As if that would ever be possible. He took his wife’s hand and they walked quickly back to the car. Without a word, Frank turned the Camry around and started back down the dirt road. He glanced in his rearview mirror just once and looked quickly away. For an instant he thought he’d seen James Dean’s face lit up on a big screen. Kristi DeMeester's storytelling voice is without parallel in today's speculative fiction field. Every story she writes feels intensely personal, cutting with an emotional edge sharp enough to flay your soul, but with such poetry and finesse you never want it to stop. Her short story collection Everything That's Underneath is essential reading, and her first novel, Beneath , is simply one of the most beautifully despairing works I've ever read. I'm certain many readers (including myself) are eagerly looking forward to her second novel, Such A Pretty Smile , coming soon from St. Martin's Press.
